results.possibleIdentity: Edward Moley (frequently published as E. Moley) was an electronics engineer specializing in microwave technology at the Airborne Instruments Laboratory (AIL), a division of Cutler-Hammer, Inc. During the 1960s and early 1970s, he was based in Long Island, New York, specifically at AIL's facilities in Deer Park and Oakdale (the latter located at the historic Idle Hour estate). Moley's technical expertise focused on the development of microwave components, including Ku-band nondegenerate parametric amplifiers, tunable reactance amplifiers, and high-power microwave electronics. He frequently collaborated with colleague James Whelehan, co-authoring several technical papers and reports for the IEEE and the Defense Technical Information Center (DTIC). Moley was a member of the technical registry for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization founded to foster collaborations between the scientific and artistic communities. His E.A.T. technical service index card identifies him as an engineer from Oakdale, NY, with expertise in microwaves, reflecting the organization's efforts to pair artists with specialists in cutting-edge technology during the late 20th century.
